votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, handling all cases (empty, singleton, and general lists) and accurately translating the sorting and permutation checks.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, handling the base cases and the parity-based sorting condition identically.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, translating the pattern matching to length checks, permutation to sorted equality, and the evenness check to modulo arithmetic.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. is_sorted_ascending and is_sorted_descending check adjacent elements which is equivalent to the Coq definitions for all i<j pairs. is_permutation using sorted comparison is equivalent to count_occ equality. The main sort_array_spec handles empty, singleton, and general cases identically, using (first + last) % 2 == 0 which matches Z.even (first + last) for the parity check.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both check: (1) empty array returns empty, (2) single element returns that element, (3) otherwise result must be a permutation of array, and if first+last is even, result is sorted descending, else ascending. The sorting predicates check the same conditions (adjacent element comparisons suffice for transitivity), permutation checking via sorted comparison is equivalent to count_occ equality, and Z.even checks the same condition as (first + last) % 2 == 0.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. is_sorted_ascending and is_sorted_descending check adjacent elements which is equivalent to the Coq definitions for all i<j pairs. is_permutation using sorted comparison is equivalent to count_occ equality. The main sort_array_spec handles empty, singleton, and general cases identically, using (first + last) % 2 == 0 which matches Z.even (first + last) for the sorting direction decision.

Base cases match; permutation via sorted equality matches count-based definition; ascending/descending via adjacent comparisons is equivalent to the Coq pairwise condition; parity check on first+last matches Z.even, including negatives.
Permutation via sorted equality matches count-based definition; ascending/descending via adjacent comparisons matches Coq’s all-pairs condition; parity check and edge cases (empty/singleton) align.

Empty and singleton cases match; first/last parity check matches; permutation via sorted equality matches count_occ; and ascending/descending checks via adjacent comparisons are equivalent to Coq’s all-pairs definition over integers due to transitivity.
